text.title: Trunk-Based Branch by Abstraction Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules grounding the trunk-based-branch-by-abstraction methodology. Each pairs a binary check + sourced rationale.

rule.statement: BbA MUST follow the five steps in order: (1) create Protocol/interface, (2) wrap old implementation, (3) implement new code behind interface, (4) flag-gated swap + validate, (5) remove old + collapse abstraction. Skipping or reordering steps is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Each step is independently shippable. Out-of-order steps leave trunk in a broken or half-migrated state; skipping step 5 leaves permanent indirection.

rule.statement: A step-5 cleanup ticket MUST be filed at step-1 merge time, linked to the feature flag. Filing the ticket later is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Cleanup tickets filed later are filed never. Agents move on; abstractions calcify into permanent layers.

rule.statement: Each BbA migration MUST use exactly one feature flag named per the project convention (e.g., billing.use_stripe_v2). Reusing an existing flag for a different BbA is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Flag reuse blurs ownership; cleanup becomes ambiguous; rollback affects unrelated paths.

rule.statement: After step 4 validation, the old implementation MUST be deletable by deleting only the wrapper + concrete class + flag block. If deletion requires editing call sites, the abstraction was not properly introduced.
rule.rationale: Step 5 should be mechanical; manual edits to call sites mean the abstraction leaked, and the migration is not safely complete.

rule.statement: Each PR MUST advance exactly one BbA step. Mixing step-2 (wrap old) with step-3 (implement new) in the same PR is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Step-mixing inflates diff size and makes failures hard to bisect; one-step-per-PR keeps each diff reviewable and revertable.

rule.statement: Step 4 (flag-gated swap) MUST include at least one dark-launch period (< 1% traffic, full diurnal cycle) before scaling. Direct 100% flip is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Dark launch catches data-shape bugs (float precision, ordering, null handling) that pass unit tests but fail on real traffic.
